prompt:
The Book of Godfrey

Chapter 1

1:1 Now there came unto the forge of Godfrey, a blacksmith of surly countenance and calloused hands, a stranger bearing a question most peculiar upon his lips.

1:2 And the stranger drew near to the anvil where Godfrey labored, amid the heat of the coals and the ringing of iron, and spoke, saying, "What is this thing called WiFi?"

1:3 Then Godfrey set down his hammer upon the anvil and turned his face toward the stranger, and his brow was furrowed as a field in planting season, and his eyes burned with the weariness of one who suffers fools.

1:4 And Godfrey opened his mouth and spoke, saying, "WiFi? What manner of word is this you bring into my forge? Is it a name? Is it some tongue of the Moors or the Norsemen? For I have never heard such a sound in all my years upon this earth."

1:5 "I have shod horses and forged swords. I have mended plowshares and beaten iron into rings of mail. I have labored since before the sun rose and will labor until it sets, and in all my days, no man has spoken this word to me."

1:6 And his anger kindled like the bellows upon the coals, and he said, "Have you come to mock me, stranger? For I have no patience for riddles and the idle prattle of those who have no iron to mend."

1:7 "If it is a tool, I have not forged it. If it is a metal, I have not smelted it. And if it is some enchantment or sorcery, then take it to the priest or to the cunning woman at the edge of the village, for I will have no part of such things."

1:8 Then Godfrey lifted his hammer once more and turned again to his anvil, saying, "Now leave me to my work, for this horseshoe will not shape itself, and the lord of the manor demands it by evening."

1:9 "WiFi," he muttered into the smoke and the sparks. "The world grows stranger with each passing day, and the people in it stranger still."
response:
This is excellent. It nails the mock-biblical cadence while grounding the joke in Godfrey’s very practical blacksmith worldview.

What works especially well:
- The faux-scriptural verse structure is immediately effective.
- “his brow was furrowed as a field in planting season” is a strong period-flavored simile.
- Godfrey’s escalating irritation feels consistent and funny.
- The contrast between medieval labor and modern terminology lands cleanly.
- “If it is a tool, I have not forged it…” is probably the strongest line.

If you want, I can help in a few ways:
1. Continue into Chapter 2 in the same style.
2. Polish this for even more King James/Biblical flavor.
3. Expand it into a longer comic scripture.
4. Write “The Gospel of WiFi,” where someone attempts to explain the internet to Godfrey.
5. Add other absurd modern intrusions: passwords, Bluetooth, QR codes, cryptocurrency, etc.

For example, here is a slightly heightened version of your passage that keeps your idea but leans a touch more into the biblical register:

---

The Book of Godfrey

Chapter 1

1:1 And it came to pass, at the forge of Godfrey, a smith of сурly countenance and hands hardened by labor, that there came unto him a stranger with a question strange and grievous upon his lips.

1:2 And the stranger drew nigh unto the anvil where Godfrey wrought amidst the heat of the coals and the clamor of iron, and he spake, saying, What is this thing called WiFi?

1:3 Then Godfrey laid down his hammer upon the anvil, and turned his face toward the man; and his brow was furrowed as a field prepared for sowing, and his eyes were full of the weariness of him that endureth fools.

1:4 And Godfrey answered and said, WiFi? What manner of word is this that thou bringest into my forge? Is it a name, or the speech of some far country? For never in all my years have mine ears received such a sound.

1:5 I have shod horses, and forged swords; I have mended plowshares, and beaten iron into rings of mail; I have labored from before the rising of the sun even unto its going down; yet never hath any man spoken this word before me.

1:6 And his wrath was kindled like the bellows upon the coals, and he said, Hast thou come to mock me, O stranger? For I have no patience for riddles, nor for the vain babbling of them that have no iron to mend.

1:7 If it be a tool, I have not forged it; if it be a metal, I have not smelted it; and if it be some enchantment or sorcery, then take it unto the priest, or unto the wise woman that dwelleth at the edge of the village; for I will have no part in it.

1:8 Then lifted Godfrey up his hammer once more, and turned again unto his anvil, saying, Depart now, and leave me unto my labor; for this horseshoe shall not shape itself, and the lord of the manor requireth it ere evening.

1:9 And he muttered amidst the smoke and the sparks, saying, WiFi. Surely the world groweth stranger with every passing day, and the people therein stranger still.

If you want, I can write Chapter 2 immediately.
